essential_question: What does 'runs forever' feel like when you build it?
objective: Physically build a point, segment, ray, and line; measure the segment; and explain why a ray and a line cannot be measured.

agenda.title: Brief — Today You Build the Four

content.heading: From definitions to string
bullets: Yesterday: named the four figures by their endpoints.
Today: make each one real with tacks + string.
The test: can you build a ray you can walk toward — and say why you can't measure it?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Lay out your board, tacks, string, ruler. Decide where P, C–D, A–B, and E–F will go so they don't overlap.

content.heading: Make them
bullets: Segment CD — string between two tacks; measure it.
Ray AB — one endpoint, string off one edge, 'forever' flag.
Line EF — string off both edges, flags both ends.

content.preview: Session 3: defend the difference in words, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can build a point, segment, ray, and line and label each correctly
I can measure a segment and record its length with units
I can explain why a ray and a line have no measurable length

questions.prompt: Measure your segment CD with the ruler. Record its length (with units).
questions.answer: Any correct measured length with a unit, e.g. 18 cm.
questions.explain: Rubric: a number + a unit (cm/in). A segment has two endpoints, so it has a real, finite length.

questions.prompt: Try to measure your ray AB. Write what happens and why in one sentence.
questions.answer: You can't — a ray runs forever past B, so it has no endpoint on that side and no finite length.
questions.explain: A ray has only one endpoint; the other direction never stops, so there is nothing to measure to.

questions.prompt: On your board you strung opposite rays from A. Name them both (endpoint first).
questions.answer: Ray AB and ray A(reverse) — e.g. ray AB and ray AX, both starting at A, pointing opposite ways.
questions.explain: Opposite rays share the endpoint A and together form a straight line.

questions.prompt: Reflect: which of the four figures was hardest to build honestly, and what did building it teach you that the definition didn't?
